mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a morphism in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}; if the induced functor { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } } between categories of elements has a right adjoint, then { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } is representable.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let there be a right adjoint { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } to { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } }, and fix { \mathopen {} \left ( \Gamma \vdash A \right ) \mathclose {}} \in \widetilde { U }. Applying the right adjoint, we have an element { \mathopen {} \left ( \Gamma .A \vdash qA \right ) \mathclose {}} \in \widetilde { E }; instantiating the counit at { \mathopen {} \left ( \Gamma \vdash A \right ) \mathclose {}}, we have a cell { \widetilde { p } qA } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon _{A} }}{ A }, which amounts to a commuting square in the following configuration:

It suffices to show that the square depicted above is cartesian. Because pullbacks in presheaf categories are computed pointwise, it suffices to consider only squares with a representable apex, such as the one below in which \Delta is assumed representable:

To construct the universal map { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ { \mathopen {} \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle \mathclose {}} }}{ \Gamma .A } factoring \gamma ,a through qA,p_A respectively, we note the square above corresponds precisely to a morphism { \widetilde { p } a } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ A } in \widetilde { U }, visualised as follows:
We can transpose { \widetilde { p } a } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ A } to obtain the following { a } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma ^ \sharp }}{ qA } in \widetilde { E }:

We now define { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ { \mathopen {} \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle \mathclose {}} }}{ \Gamma .A } to be the image of { a } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma ^ \sharp }}{ qA } in \mathcal {C}, i.e. { \mathopen {} \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle \mathclose {}} : \equiv \pi _E \bar \gamma ^ \sharp.The factorisation qA \circ { \mathopen {} \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle \mathclose {}} = a holds by inspection of the above diagram in \widetilde { E }. The other factorisation p_A \circ { \mathopen {} \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle \mathclose {}} = \gamma holds by the snake identities of adjoints:

Uniqueness of { a } \xrightarrow {{ { \mathopen {} \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle \mathclose {}} }}{ qA } with this property can be seen as follows. We fix any { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ h }}{ \Gamma .A } satisfying the two factorisation properties; in other words, we assume that h lies underneath a morphism in { a } \xrightarrow {{ \bar {h} }}{ qA } in \widetilde { E } satisfying (only) the additional property that \epsilon _A \circ \bar {h} = \gamma:
To see that h = { \mathopen {} \left \langle \gamma ,a \right \rangle \mathclose {}}, it therefore suffices to observe the following instance of the snake identity:

frontmatter.title: The adjunction determined by a representable map
The generic elements functor of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}. We shall define the generic elements functor { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } that, intuitively, sends an element of the base to the “generic element” of the fiber of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } at that element.

li.p: Fixing an element \Gamma \vdash A, we define qA to be the upstairs map in the following pullback square that exists by representability of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }, and can be chosen by the univalence principle for the category \mathcal {C}:

Fixing a morphism { A } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ B } in \widetilde { U } over { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ \Gamma } in \mathcal {C}, we choose { qA } \xrightarrow {{ q \bar \gamma }}{ qB } to be the upper triangle determined by the universal property of the pullback as follows:

Commutation of the outer square follows from the homomorphism property of { A } \xrightarrow {{ \bar \gamma }}{ B } in \widetilde { U }. The assignment preserves identities and compositions, so we have defined a functor { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } }.

frontmatter.title: The weakening counit of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}, so that { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the corresponding generic elements functor. We may define a natural transformation { \widetilde { p } \circ q } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon }}{ 1 _{ \widetilde { U } } } that we shall call the weakening counit of the representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }.
For \Gamma \vdash A we define the component { \widetilde { p } qA } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon _A }}{ A } to be the following cell over { \Gamma .A } \xrightarrow {{ p_A }}{ A }:

The reason to name \epsilon _A as we have done is that it witnesses the fact that “weakening” a given type \Gamma \vdash A into the context of its generic element gives you precisely the type of the generic element.

frontmatter.title: The substitution unit of a representable map

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}, so that { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the corresponding generic elements functor. We may define a natural transformation { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } that we shall call the substitution unit of the representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }.
We define { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } assigning to each \Gamma \vdash a : A the upper-hand depicted below:

The reason to refer to { 1 _{ \widetilde { E } } } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ q \circ \widetilde { p } } as the substitution unit is that it witnesses the fact that every element of a given type arises from that type’s generic element by substitution.

frontmatter.title: Snake identity: duplicating the generic element

mainmatter.p: The following “snake identity” for the substitution unit and the weakening counit holds for any representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}:
As we shall see in the proof below, this identity states that duplicating the generic element gives you the generic element (up to weakening).

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We first instantiate the snake diagram at an arbitrary { \mathopen {} \left ( \Gamma \vdash A \right ) \mathclose {}} \in \widetilde { U }.

As { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \pi _E }}{ \mathcal {C} } is a discrete fibration, it suffices to check that the above diagram lies over an identity cell in \mathcal {C}. We therefore consider its image in \mathcal {C} as an endomorphism on \Gamma .A to check that it is the identity, computing as follows using the componentwise definitions of the substitution unit and the weakening counit:

These two maps evidently compose to the identity: indeed, by the universal property of \Gamma .A as the fiber product E \times _U \Gamma, it suffices to check that qA \circ p_A.qpqA \circ { \mathopen {} \left \langle \Gamma .A,qA \right \rangle \mathclose {}} = qA and p_A \circ p_A.qpqA \circ { \mathopen {} \left \langle \Gamma .A,qA \right \rangle \mathclose {}} = p_A, as can be read off from the diagrams below.

mainmatter.p: The following “snake identity” for the substitution unit and the weakening counit holds for any representable map { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C}:
As we shall see, this law states that any element \Gamma \vdash a : A gives rise to a section of the display map { \Gamma .A } \xrightarrow {{ p_A }}{ \Gamma }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We may instantiate the snake diagram at an arbitrary { \mathopen {} \left ( \Gamma \vdash a:A \right ) \mathclose {}} \in \widetilde { E }, and we may whisker on the right with { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ \pi _U }}{ \mathcal {C} } which, as a discrete fibration, is a faithful functor. It suffices now to check that the resulting cell is an identity map \mathcal {C}. We we compute it below using the componentwise definitions of the substitution unit and weakening counit.

The cell depicted above is indeed an identity, as { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ \Gamma .a }}{ \Gamma .A } is defined to be the section of { \Gamma .A } \xrightarrow {{ p_A }}{ \Gamma } determined by { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ a }}{ E }.

frontmatter.title: Adjointness of the generic elements functor

mainmatter.p: Let { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a representable map in \operatorname {Pr} { \mathcal {C} }; then we have an adjunction \widetilde { p } \dashv q where { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } is the generic elements functor of { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U }, in which the unit and counit are the substitution unit and weakening counit respectively.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We have verified the two snake identities in Lemmas [jms-00DP] and [jms-00DQ] respectively.

mainmatter.p: A morphism { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } is representable in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C} if and only if the induced functor { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } } has a right adjoint.
